coredata.dc:title: Amorphous carbon layer: An effective assistant for realizing near-infrared-activated photocatalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanyuan
Wang, Lili
Ma, Xiumei
Yang, Manli
Jiang, Haiyan
Li, Lin
Yuan, Chuancong
Shi, Jinsheng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were considered as desirable up-conversion luminescent materials. However, the reason leading to their up-conversion luminescence was not clear. In this paper, CQDs decorated ZnSn(OH)6 was successfully synthesized by facile in situ hydrothermal method. Under NIR light irradiation, ciprofloxacin degradation efficiency of sample was 37.4%. Subsequently, reasons for the up-conversion effect of CQDs were investigated to some extent. An amorphous carbon layer around CQDs was observed in composite (ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs@C) by HR-TEM and elemental mapping images. To study the effect of amorphous carbon layer on up-conversion performance, individual CQDs decorated ZnSn(OH)6 (ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs) was also fabricated by compositely coating. ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs had no up-conversion luminescence under 980 nm laser light excitation and its photocatalytic activity was negligible, implying amorphous carbon layer played a crucial role for realizing of up-conversion luminescence. A comparison of the FTIR spectra of two composites, ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs@C sample revealed greatly enhanced surface oxidation degree, polarity and hydrophilicity. Surface state of ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs@C composite was controlled by adjusting hydrothermal time, and the results confirmed that up-conversion performance had a close relationship with surface states of samples. This work could provide a new insight into understanding the up-conversion effect of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Amorphous carbon layer
Up-conversion reason
ZnSn(OH)6
Near-infrared-activated photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The relationship between UC luminescence and surface states
Photocatalytic activity of samples under different light irradiation condition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Controlled fabrication processes for (a) ZSH@CQDs@C and (b) ZSH@CQDs.
SEM images of as-prepared different samples.

TEM images of (a) ZSH@CQDs@C yolk-shell, (b) cross-sectional compositional line profile of ZSH@CQDs@C with yolk-shell structure (green line for C, red line for Sn and yellow line for Zn); (c-f) TEM images and HR-TEM images of CQDs and amorphous carbon layer in ZSH@CQDs@C yolk-shell. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Elemental mapping images of (a) Zn-K, (b) Sn-K, (c) O-K and (d) C-K of ZSH@CQDs@C.

(a) UC emission spectra of obtained samples under 980 nm laser light excitation; (b) FTIR spectra of different samples; (c) UC emission spectra and (d) FTIR spectra of ZSH@CQDS@C composite under different hydrothermal treatment time.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of different samples; (b) DTA curves of as-prepared samples.
XRD patterns of as-prepared different catalysts.
XPS spectra of synthesized ZSH@CQDs@C catalyst: (a) survey spectrum, (b) Zn 2p, (c) Sn 3d and (d) C 1s.

Photocatalytic degradation with different samples under (a) full-spectrum light, (b) visible light and (c) 980 nm laser light irradiation, (d) photocatalytic degradation of CIP over ZSH@CQDs@C under 980 nm laser light irradiation in the presence of different scavengers.
Schematic illustration of the possible photocatalytic processes in the ZSH@CQDs@C composite under 980 nm light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Amorphous carbon layer: An effective assistant for realizing near-infrared-activated photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science, Qingdao Agricultural University, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingdao Bona Biomimetic Composite Research Institute Co. Ltd., PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingdao Bona Biomimetic Composite Research Institute Co. Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electron Microscopy Laboratory, School of Physics, Peking University, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Qingdao Agricultural University, Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science, Chengyang District, Qingdao, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were considered as desirable up-conversion luminescent materials. However, the reason leading to their up-conversion luminescence was not clear. In this paper, CQDs decorated ZnSn(OH)6 was successfully synthesized by facile in situ hydrothermal method. Under NIR light irradiation, ciprofloxacin degradation efficiency of sample was 37.4%. Subsequently, reasons for the up-conversion effect of CQDs were investigated to some extent. An amorphous carbon layer around CQDs was observed in composite (ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs@C) by HR-TEM and elemental mapping images. To study the effect of amorphous carbon layer on up-conversion performance, individual CQDs decorated ZnSn(OH)6 (ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs) was also fabricated by compositely coating. ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs had no up-conversion luminescence under 980 nm laser light excitation and its photocatalytic activity was negligible, implying amorphous carbon layer played a crucial role for realizing of up-conversion luminescence. A comparison of the FTIR spectra of two composites, ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs@C sample revealed greatly enhanced surface oxidation degree, polarity and hydrophilicity. Surface state of ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs@C composite was controlled by adjusting hydrothermal time, and the results confirmed that up-conversion performance had a close relationship with surface states of samples. This work could provide a new insight into understanding the up-conversion effect of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid development of social economy, especially acceleration of the process of industrialization, wastewater pollution has become a great threat to aquatic life, human survival and social development [1–3]. Millions of serious illness and even death are caused every year by them [4]. Therefore, it is urgently needed to develop rapid and efficient water purification techniques to solve this global challenge [5]. Photocatalysis, as an efficient, low cost and environmentally friendly strategy, has been emerged a preferable candidate in comparison with traditional purification methods [6,7].

In recent years, perovskite materials have attracted widespread interest in organic pollutants photodegradation as well as water splitting for hydrogen production [8,9]. Zinc hydroxystannate (ZnSn(OH)6, ZSH), as one of the perovskite materials, is tended to generate face-centered-cubic crystal structure and filled with hydroxyl groups on surface [10,11]. Much attention has been paid to potential applications of ZSH in photocatalytic process due to its high and stable activity. The efficient photocatalytic performance is assigned to abundant surface OH− groups, which utilize photoinduced holes to form highly active
OH radicals. The simple regeneration process of
OH adducts includes participation of
O2 − and H2O [12]. Unfortunately, ZSH could only utilize UV light owing to its wide band gap of 4.0 eV, which only occupies 5% of solar spectrum, whereas residual 45% visible light and 50% NIR light could not be utilized effectively [13]. Among various modified method, morphology control and up-conversion materials decoration could be two effective strategies. On the one hand, based on the reported papers, materials with yolk-shell structures displayed strengthened photocatalytic activity in comparison with hollow and solid counterparts owing to their multi-reflections of light, organic pollutant excellent and light adsorption efficiency [14–16]. On the other hand, carbon is a readily available and low-cost material with nontoxic benefits, which is usually utilized as both supports and metal-free co-catalysts [17,18]. Recently, CQDs, as a newly developed zero-dimensional nanomaterial with sp2 carbon structure, have been widely applied in photocatalysis [19,20]. Compared to traditional up-conversion (UC) materials doped with rare earth ions, light response of CQDs is not limited to a specific wavelength because of their wide cross-section of UC absorption and a large range of emission spectra that promote energy band-gap matching [21–23]. The unique characters of CQDs such as excellent electrical conductivity, high aqueous solubility, tunable photoluminescence (PL) emissions, environmental friendliness, high chemical stability and earth abundance enable their applications in nanomedicine, bioimaging, biosensing and photocatalysis [24–27]. In particular, CQDs are excellent spectral harvesters and converters due to a wide range of UC absorption and a large cross-section of PL emission spectra, which could convert NIR light to visible and/or UV light through multiple photon absorption [28–30]. In addition, CQDs are also demonstrated to be an excellent electron reservoir to capture photogenerated electrons and inhibit recombination of charge carriers, thereby increasing their lifetimes [24,31].

Herein, ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs and ZnSn(OH)6@CQDs@C microspheres with yolk-shell structure were successfully synthesized by compositely coating and in situ hydrothermal method, respectively. PL spectra proved that individual CQDs have no UC character toward 980 nm laser light. Interestingly, self-assembly amorphous carbon could assist CQDs to realize UC luminescence and further driven ZSH-based photocatalytic activity. FTIR and PL spectra suggested that surface states of samples might control the up-conversion luminescence performance This work could provide a new insight into understanding the up-conversion effect of CQDs.

ZSH sample with yolk-shell structure was prepared through a facile surfactant-assistant solvothermal method and etching-second growth strategy. The detailed description is presented in Supporting Information. Subsequently, the obtained ZSH as precursor was utilized to fabricate ZSH@CQDs@C composite (Fig. 1 a). Typically, 100 mg of ZSH were dispersed into 40 mL deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. After that, 360 mg glucose and 35 mg ascorbic acid were added into the above dispersion with magnetic stir for 30 min. Then the hybrids were transferred to 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, precipitate was collected by centrifuge and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol. Final products were dried at 60 °C overnight for further characterization.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.07.041.

ZSH sample with yolk-shell structure was prepared through a facile surfactant-assistant solvothermal method and etching-second growth strategy. The detailed description is presented in Supporting Information . Subsequently, the obtained ZSH as precursor was utilized to fabricate ZSH@CQDs@C composite (Fig. 1 a). Typically, 100 mg of ZSH were dispersed into 40 mL deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. After that, 360 mg glucose and 35 mg ascorbic acid were added into the above dispersion with magnetic stir for 30 min. Then the hybrids were transferred to 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, precipitate was collected by centrifuge and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol. Final products were dried at 60 °C overnight for further characterization.

Pure CQDs (only using 360 mg of glucose and 35 mg of ascorbic acid) was fabricated by the same method. ZSH@CQDs composite was also synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method (Fig. 1b). The detailed description is presented in Supporting Information.

Pure CQDs (only using 360 mg of glucose and 35 mg of ascorbic acid) was fabricated by the same method. ZSH@CQDs composite was also synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method (Fig. 1b). The detailed description is presented in Supporting Information.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are useful technologies to investigate morphology and microstructure of as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 2 a, ZSH precursor spheres with a smooth surface is uniform and the average size is about 1.3 μm. TEM image in Fig. S1a confirms their solid structure. In the formation of ZSH with yolk-shell structure, ZSH dissolution mainly happen between passivated outer surface of pre-grown seed particles and repeatedly deposited crystal layers, resulting in the generation of yolk-shell structure. As can be seen from Fig. 2b, solid spheres as seed could be preserved well after generation of new shell. In addition, as shown in Fig. S1b, obvious contrast of dark edges and pale center can be clearly seen, suggesting yolk-shell internal structure with a solid yolk. In construction process of CQDs decorated ZSH samples, glucose and ascorbic acid are utilized as a carbon source and acid catalyst for synthesis of CQDs, respectively. Fig. S1c presents the HR-TEM image of CQDs and their average diameters are about 4–5 nm. SEM image in Fig. 2c and TEM images in S1d suggest uniform spherical structure preserved well after modification of CQDs. When ZSH, glucose and ascorbic acid are mixed together, both CQDs and self-assembly amorphous carbon layers are simultaneously formed and surround ZSH microspheres after hydrothermal process. SEM images (Fig. 2d) and TEM images (Fig. 3 a) of ZSH@CQDs@C suggest that uniform sphere morphology is perfectly maintained after modification of double carbon layers. Fig. 3b are cross-sectional compositional line profile of ZSH@CQDs@C, which further confirms the yolk-shell structure. Besides, in Fig. 3c, it could be clearly observed that ZSH sample is encapsulated by a self-assembly CQDs layer and intrinsic morphology of CQDs is provided by their HR-TEM images (Fig. 3d). Lattice spacing of CQDs is 0.23 nm and agrees well with (1 0 0) lattice planes of graphite, suggesting CQDs have a crystalline graphitic carbon structure. Furthermore, another amorphous carbon layer arranged over CQDs layer could be clearly seen in Fig. 3e and f. No interlayer gaps between ZSH, CQDs and carbon layers, confirming that CQDs and amorphous carbon layers are strongly attached to ZSH.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are useful technologies to investigate morphology and microstructure of as-prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 2 a, ZSH precursor spheres with a smooth surface is uniform and the average size is about 1.3 μm. TEM image in Fig. S1a confirms their solid structure. In the formation of ZSH with yolk-shell structure, ZSH dissolution mainly happen between passivated outer surface of pre-grown seed particles and repeatedly deposited crystal layers, resulting in the generation of yolk-shell structure. As can be seen from Fig. 2b, solid spheres as seed could be preserved well after generation of new shell. In addition, as shown in Fig. S1b, obvious contrast of dark edges and pale center can be clearly seen, suggesting yolk-shell internal structure with a solid yolk. In construction process of CQDs decorated ZSH samples, glucose and ascorbic acid are utilized as a carbon source and acid catalyst for synthesis of CQDs, respectively. Fig. S1c presents the HR-TEM image of CQDs and their average diameters are about 4–5 nm. SEM image in Fig. 2c and TEM images in S1d suggest uniform spherical structure preserved well after modification of CQDs. When ZSH, glucose and ascorbic acid are mixed together, both CQDs and self-assembly amorphous carbon layers are simultaneously formed and surround ZSH microspheres after hydrothermal process. SEM images (Fig. 2d) and TEM images (Fig. 3 a) of ZSH@CQDs@C suggest that uniform sphere morphology is perfectly maintained after modification of double carbon layers. Fig. 3b are cross-sectional compositional line profile of ZSH@CQDs@C, which further confirms the yolk-shell structure. Besides, in Fig. 3c, it could be clearly observed that ZSH sample is encapsulated by a self-assembly CQDs layer and intrinsic morphology of CQDs is provided by their HR-TEM images (Fig. 3d). Lattice spacing of CQDs is 0.23 nm and agrees well with (1 0 0) lattice planes of graphite, suggesting CQDs have a crystalline graphitic carbon structure. Furthermore, another amorphous carbon layer arranged over CQDs layer could be clearly seen in Fig. 3e and f. No interlayer gaps between ZSH, CQDs and carbon layers, confirming that CQDs and amorphous carbon layers are strongly attached to ZSH.

TEM-mapping was investigated to confirm elemental distribution of sample. Fig. 4 shows a single sphere-like composite and corresponding elements distribution including Zn, Sn, O, and C. As can be seen from Fig. 4a–c, elements of Zn, Sn and O are homogeneously distributed throughout the single micro-sphere and an obvious yolk-shell could be clearly observed. In Fig. 4d, appreciable amount of C element almost uniformly distributes in the surface and round of ZSH, which is ascribed to CQDs, as well as the amorphous carbon layer. However, surrounding carbon element is almost invisible when individual CQDs are utilized to decorate ZSH, meaning amorphous carbon layer is absent in composites (Fig. S2). These results demonstrate that amorphous carbon layer spontaneously generates in the formation of ZSH@CQDs@C composite.

TEM-mapping was investigated to confirm elemental distribution of sample. Fig. 4 shows a single sphere-like composite and corresponding elements distribution including Zn, Sn, O, and C. As can be seen from Fig. 4a–c, elements of Zn, Sn and O are homogeneously distributed throughout the single micro-sphere and an obvious yolk-shell could be clearly observed. In Fig. 4d, appreciable amount of C element almost uniformly distributes in the surface and round of ZSH, which is ascribed to CQDs, as well as the amorphous carbon layer. However, surrounding carbon element is almost invisible when individual CQDs are utilized to decorate ZSH, meaning amorphous carbon layer is absent in composites (Fig. S2). These results demonstrate that amorphous carbon layer spontaneously generates in the formation of ZSH@CQDs@C composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The relationship between UC luminescence and surface states
ce:section.ce:para: PL is one of the most attractive characters of materials [32]. Fig. S3 and 5 a provide PL spectra of catalysts under visible and NIR light, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, both ZSH@CQDs and ZSH@CQDs@C exhibit effective UC performance toward visible light (500–800 nm) and their emission bands in UV region are further used by ZSH. However, when excitation wavelength increases to 980 nm, individual CQDs decorated ZSH composite present no emission band in the range of 200–500 nm. Interestingly, as for ZSH@CQDs@C composite, amorphous carbon layer could assist CQDs to realize UC luminescence and its emission bands locate in both UV and visible regions (Fig. 5a). FTIR spectroscopy is a useful technique to investigate the different types of functional groups and chemical linkages after the coating of carbon material. As for CQDs, main stretching bands located at 3195, 1718 1164 and 775 cm−1, which determine existence of OH, CO, CO and H2OH2O hydrogen binding absorption in its structure, respectively [33]. These functional groups and chemical linkages are also observed in FTIR spectra of carbon material decorated composites. Besides, two vibrations of Sn involved bonds at 540 and 648 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching modes of SnOH and SnOSn, respectively [34]. Spectral band at 775 cm−1 could be corresponded to H2OH2O hydrogen binding absorption [13]. In Fig. 5b, a comparison of the FTIR spectra of ZSH@CQDs and ZSH@CQDs@C samples reveal two important observations. One is an increase of the typical stretching vibrations of CO bond at 1718 cm−1 and CO bond at 1164 cm−1 from ZSH@CQDs to ZSH@CQDs@C, confirming an enhancement in the degree of oxidation [35]. The other is that the O-H vibration bands at about 3195 cm−1 are sharp and discrete for ZSH@CQDs but broad and integrated for ZSH@CQDs@C. In general, a broad O-H band suggests multiple structures of hydroxyl groups on the nanoparticle surfaces, leading to higher polarity and hydrophilicity of ZSH@CQDs@C in comparison with ZSH@CQDs [36]. Although the UC luminescence mechanisms for amorphous carbon assisted CQDs remain unclear. However, based on the reported paper, surface oxidation may create surface defects, which could cause surface-state-related fluorescence. A higher degree of oxidation on the CQDs surfaces implies more surface defects [37]. Therefore, it is reasonably deduced that surface states could control the PL features [38].

PL is one of the most attractive characters of materials [32]. Fig. S3 and 5 a provide PL spectra of catalysts under visible and NIR light, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, both ZSH@CQDs and ZSH@CQDs@C exhibit effective UC performance toward visible light (500–800 nm) and their emission bands in UV region are further used by ZSH. However, when excitation wavelength increases to 980 nm, individual CQDs decorated ZSH composite present no emission band in the range of 200–500 nm. Interestingly, as for ZSH@CQDs@C composite, amorphous carbon layer could assist CQDs to realize UC luminescence and its emission bands locate in both UV and visible regions (Fig. 5a). FTIR spectroscopy is a useful technique to investigate the different types of functional groups and chemical linkages after the coating of carbon material. As for CQDs, main stretching bands located at 3195, 1718 1164 and 775 cm−1, which determine existence of OH, CO, CO and H2OH2O hydrogen binding absorption in its structure, respectively [33]. These functional groups and chemical linkages are also observed in FTIR spectra of carbon material decorated composites. Besides, two vibrations of Sn involved bonds at 540 and 648 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching modes of SnOH and SnOSn, respectively [34]. Spectral band at 775 cm−1 could be corresponded to H2OH2O hydrogen binding absorption [13]. In Fig. 5b, a comparison of the FTIR spectra of ZSH@CQDs and ZSH@CQDs@C samples reveal two important observations. One is an increase of the typical stretching vibrations of CO bond at 1718 cm−1 and CO bond at 1164 cm−1 from ZSH@CQDs to ZSH@CQDs@C, confirming an enhancement in the degree of oxidation [35]. The other is that the O-H vibration bands at about 3195 cm−1 are sharp and discrete for ZSH@CQDs but broad and integrated for ZSH@CQDs@C. In general, a broad O-H band suggests multiple structures of hydroxyl groups on the nanoparticle surfaces, leading to higher polarity and hydrophilicity of ZSH@CQDs@C in comparison with ZSH@CQDs [36]. Although the UC luminescence mechanisms for amorphous carbon assisted CQDs remain unclear. However, based on the reported paper, surface oxidation may create surface defects, which could cause surface-state-related fluorescence. A higher degree of oxidation on the CQDs surfaces implies more surface defects [37]. Therefore, it is reasonably deduced that surface states could control the PL features [38].

In order to further demonstrate the speculation, a verified experiment was carried out. In Fig. 5c, with the increase of hydrothermal treatment time from 6 to 24 h, the OH vibration bands become broad and integrated, indicating the longer hydrothermal treatment time, the higher polarity and hydrophilicity on the surface of CQDs. In addition, peak intensities of stretching vibration ascribing to CO bonds and CO enhance, implying that an enhancement in the degree of oxidation could occur with the increase of treatment time. Meanwhile, as can be seen from Fig. 5d, ZSH@CQDS@C composite presents negligible UC luminescence intensities when treatment time is 6 h. However, PL intensities gradually enhance with the increase of hydrothermal time, consisting with above-mentioned results.

Optical absorption performance of materials is a vital factor to influence photocatalytic activity [39]. Fig. 6 a presents UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of as-prepared pure samples, together with that of carbon materials modified ZSH. No absorption could be observed for pure ZSH within visible light region and their absorption edges locate at about 370 nm. While after decoration of CQDs, an obvious red-shift occur for composites. Compared with pure ZSH, ZSH@CQDs present strong absorption in the whole visible light region. Furthermore, when ZSH are simultaneously surrounded by CQDs and amorphous carbon layers, light absorption of sample could further enhance. These results suggest that amorphous carbon layer could assist CQDs to strengthen light adsorption of material.

As shown in Fig. 6b, pure ZSH presents an obvious endothermic peak in the range of 200–280 °C, attributing to the phase transition of ZSH. After decorating by carbon material, an exothermic peak between 280 and 510 °C could be clearly seen for ZSH@CQDs and ZSH@CQDs@C, ascribing to the burn of carbon layers. Besides, the exothermic peak of ZSH@CQDs is much higher than that of ZSH@CQDs@C, suggesting the difference of carbon layers in the two composites.

Phase structure of as-prepared materials was studied by XRD pattern. As shown in Fig. 7 , all diffraction peaks of samples All samples present similar diffraction patterns, which match well with cubic ZSH crystal phase (JCPDS 73-2384). This result implies that the incorporation of CQDs does not have a notable influence on the crystalline structures of samples. However, the diffraction peak of CQDs and amorphous carbon could not be observed in composites. This could be attributed to the intrinsic low crystallinity of CQDs and amorphous carbon or the low content of them, leading to it falling below the detection limit of the XRD instrument. A similar observation was also reported in the literature [21,40]. XPS analysis is a useful technique to further investigate surface chemical compositions of samples [41]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, main elements including Zn, Sn, O and C could be determined. High resolution XPS spectra of Sn 3d, Zn 2p and C 1s are exhibited in Fig. 9 b–d, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 8b, two strong peaks at binding energy values of 1045.6 eV and 1022.1 eV could be attributed to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 for Zn2+ state, respectively [13]. In Fig. 8c, element of Sn presents two characteristic peaks at 495.7 eV and 487.2 eV, which are attributed to Sn 2p3/2 and Sn 2p5/2, respectively. This result agrees well with the character of Sn4+ ion [14]. The C 1s peak could be deconvoluted to four peaks at 288.5, 287.7, 286.3 and 284.7 eV, which are corresponded to C(O)O, CO, CO and CC, respectively (Fig. 8d) [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of samples under different light irradiation condition
ce:section.ce:para: The sufficient utilization of solar spectrum has become one of the research hotspots in the photocatalytic field to both realize environmental purification and avoid secondary contamination. Fig. 9a presents the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples under the full-spectrum light irradiation. Compared with pure ZSH and CQDs decorated ZSH, ZSH@CQDs@C composite presents enhanced photocatalytic activity for ciprofloxacin (CIP) degradation and its CIP removal efficiency is 83.6%, which is about 1.5 and 2.5 folds than that of ZSH@CQDs and ZSH, respectively. In addition, photocatalytic performance of the as-synthesized catalysts was also investigated under visible light (λ > 420 nm). Similarly, a large enhancement in photocatalytic activity could be observed for ZSH@CQDs@C in comparison with ZSH and ZSH@CQDs composite (Fig. 9b). CIP degradation efficiency of ZSH@CQDs@C is 67.8%, which are 1.38 and 67.8 folds than that of ZSH@CQDs and ZSH, respectively.

It is well known that NIR light accounts for a great part of solar spectrum. According to the reported articles, CQDs were considered as desirable UC luminescence material, which convert NIR light to visible and/or UV light. Therefore, NIR light response performance of different samples was also studied toward CIP degradation under NIR light irradiation (λ = 980 nm). Compared with those under visible light or full-spectrum light, NIR light-driven photocatalytic activity largely decreases owing to the long wavelength and the relatively weak energy of NIR light. As shown in Fig. 9c, under NIR irradiation, pure ZSH and ZSH@CQDs composite exhibit negligible removal efficiencies. However, when ZSH are co-decorated by CQDs and amorphous carbon layers, photodegradation efficiency could reach to 37.4%, which is more than 30 times in comparison with their counterparts. These results suggest that individual CQDs may not convert NIR to UV light, while amorphous carbon could assist CQDs to realize UC effect and further driven ZSH-based photocatalytic activity.

In order to confirm the active adducts during photocatalytic activity under NIR light irradiation, radicals trapping experiment over ZSH@CQDs@C was carried out. Several scavengers including isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), and p-benzoquinone (BQ) are introduced into photocatalytic reaction system. As can be seen from Fig. 9d, photocatalytic activity is apparently inhibited after the introduction of IPA, EDTA-2Na and BQ as quencher of
OH, h+ and
O2 −, respectively. These results suggest that
OH, h+ and
O2 − participate in photocatalytic reaction.

On the basis of mentioned results, a potential mechanism for NIR-driven photocatalytic activity was reasonably illustrated in Fig. 10 . Experimental results demonstrate that individual CQDs have no UC ability toward 980 nm laser light. Interestingly, under NIR light illumination, self-assembly amorphous carbon layer could assist CQDs to realize UC luminescence, which could first absorb NIR light, followed by shorter wavelength light emission in the range of UV light. ZSH utilize converted UV light and generate photoinduced charge carriers. Besides, CQDs and carbon layers could also serve as an electron reservoir and photogenerated electrons could easily transfer to carbon material from CB of ZSH, inhibiting electron-hole recombination and thereby improving their lifetimes [29,43,44]. Furthermore, photogenerated electrons in carbon layers would react with O2 on surface of samples to generate active
O2 −, which is confirmed one of the main active adducts in photocatalytic process. Meanwhile, holes in VB of ZSH would transfer to its surface and then react with OH− to produce •OH, together with h+ are regarded as another two major active radicals.

In summary, ZSH@CQDs and ZSH@CQDs@C composites were successfully synthesized by compositely coating and in situ hydrothermal method, respectively. According to the reported papers, CQDs were considered as desirable UC luminescence material, which converted near infrared light to visible and/or UV light. However, in this paper, individual CQDs were found no UC character toward 980 nm laser light. Interestingly, self-assembly amorphous carbon could assist CQDs to realize effective UC luminescence and further driven ZSH-based photocatalytic activity. Under 980 nm laser light irradiation, CIP photodegradation efficiency of ZSH@CQDs@C reached to 37.4%, which was more than 30 times in comparison with ZSH@CQDs. FTIR and PL spectra suggested that surface states of samples might control the up-conversion luminescence performance This work could provide a new insight into understanding the up-conversion effect of CQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Doctoral Foundation of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, (ZR2017BEM013), Scientific Research Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (6631113317) and Scientific Research Foundation of Qingdao Agricultural University (6631111302).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photocatalytic inactivation of Escherichia coli by a novel Z-scheme g-C3N4/m-Bi2O4 hybrid photocatalyst under visible light: The role of reactive oxygen species
Appl. Catal. B, Environ.
